title: Java Abstract Class
description: Practice abstract classes and concrete overrides.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if code writes new Shape()?
choices: It creates a Shape whose area() returns 0
It creates a Shape but throws at runtime when area() is called
It creates an anonymous subclass automatically
Compilation fails because Shape is abstract and cannot be instantiated
completionChecks.explanation: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly, so new Shape() fails to compile; clients must instantiate a concrete subclass such as Circle.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if Circle removes its area() override?
choices: Circle inherits a default area() that returns 0
Circle fails to compile unless it is itself declared abstract
Circle compiles but throws AbstractMethodError when area() is called
Circle compiles because abstract methods are optional for subclasses
completionChecks.explanation: A concrete class must implement every inherited abstract method; without area() Circle is incomplete, so the compiler rejects it unless Circle is also declared abstract.

completionChecks.prompt: What does new Circle(2).area() return approximately?
choices: Compilation fails because area is abstract in Shape
completionChecks.explanation: Circle's override runs the formula Math.PI * radius * radius, so a radius of 2 gives about 12.57.
